title: Common password list appears outdated and misses modern weak passwords
author: abhayrana1701
body:
**Describe the feature request**:

The current common password list appears to miss many modern weak passwords that are now extremely common due to predictable password policy adaptation patterns.

Examples currently missing include:

* Password@123
* Welcome@123
* Admin123
* Qwerty123
* Admin@1234
* Pass@123
* User@123
* Password1!

These passwords are extremely common in real-world usage today and are often more predictable than many legacy entries currently included in the list.

Modern users frequently satisfy password complexity requirements by:

* capitalizing the first character,
* appending `123`,
* appending `@123`,
* or combining simple dictionary words with minimal modifications.

As a result, attackers commonly target these patterns during credential stuffing and brute-force attacks.

The current dataset appears heavily biased toward older leaked password corpora and legacy internet culture, while missing many modern “policy-compliant weak passwords”.

Suggested improvements:

* Refresh the dataset using newer breach corpora.
* Include modern weak password patterns.
* Add regionally common variants.
* Consider generating rule-based variants such as `Password@123`, `Admin123`, etc.

**Additional context**:

Many currently included passwords appear to originate from older datasets from early internet/forum leaks, while modern weak-password trends are not sufficiently represented.

Examples of older entries currently present:

* thx1138
* ncc1701
* ou812
* rush2112

Meanwhile, highly common modern passwords such as `Password@123` and `Admin123` are absent.

**Next steps**:

* [ ] I intend to support the project at "https://github.com/sponsors/itsignacioportal" so this issue is prioritized and resolved quickly by project maintainers.
* [ ] I intend to open a pull request later

title: Security Alert: Exposed Databento API Key in repository
author: devalexrios
body:
### Description
A secret scanning alert has detected an exposed Databento API Key in this repository. The credential is fully visible in the commit history and is flagged as a public leak.

### Location
- **Secret Type:** Databento API Key
- **Detected Path:** `Discovery/DNS/trickest-inventory-subdo...`
- **Key Prefix:** `db-appdeploy63wc...`

### Impact
Exposing API keys publicly allows unauthorized third parties to abuse the service, potentially leading to data theft, service disruption, or unexpected financial charges if the account has linked billing.

### Recommended Actions
1. **Revoke the Key:** Deactivate/delete this specific API key immediately from the Databento dashboard.
2. **Generate a New Key:** Create a new credential to replace it.
3. **Secure the Codebase:** Use environment variables (e.g., `.env` files) instead of hardcoding credentials, and ensure the configuration files are added to `.gitignore`.
4. **Purge Git History:** Use tools like `git-filter-repo` or BFG Repo-Cleaner to completely remove the compromised key from past commits.

title: Prevent github actions from executing on forks by default
author: ItsIgnacioPortal
body:
**Describe the feature request**:
As evidenced by many issues and pull requests, the automatic updates of wordlists done by github actions is frequently a problem for new contributors and people who fork the project, as they're not aware they should disable github actions on their forks. This ends up making contributing more difficult than it should be, and also puts an unnecessary burden on the github actions runner servers. I propose making all github actions flows check for a specific environment variable. If the variable is set, then the action should run; otherwise, it should not run.

**Additional context**:
See: Issue #1230

title: Add maintenance schedule
author: ItsIgnacioPortal
body:
**Describe the feature request**:
We should consider implementing a regular maintenance schedule to SecLists. Currently, every year we have to:
- Add a new "Most used passwords of the year" wordlist
- Add new years to the wordlists that rely on the current year
- Update the github actions dependency SHA hashes (See: #1297)
- Verify relevance of existing wordlists. There exist a number of wordlists within SecLists that may or may not be worth keeping in the repository considering their relevance to the modern world. Every year we should consider which wordlists should be moved to a directory of deprecated wordlists, and which should be deleted. See, for example: [Discovery/Web-Content/LEGACY-SERVICES
](https://github.com/danielmiessler/SecLists/tree/master/Discovery/Web-Content/LEGACY-SERVICES)

This could all be in a file named "MAINTENANCE-POLICY.md". Preferably with more detail than what is described here, like the filepath locations of all the wordlists that need to be updated, filepaths of the github actions that need to be updated, links, shorthand commands, etc.

- [X] I intend to open a pull request later

title: Comprehensive Windows File List
author: ghsdpolley
body:
**Describe the feature request**:
When looking for a clean, comprehensive list containing Windows files of interest (such as configs, credentials, logs, versioning, etc.), the existing SecLists wordlists were limited and often intermingled with *unix files.  I started building a 'Read Files' list to better assist in performing blind attacks (Directory Traversal, Fuzzing, LFI, SSRF, etc.) and, using SecLists as such a frequent go-to source for wordlists, wanted to submit one or two for inclusion consideration.  One of the bigger existing Windows lists in LFI contains 236 entries, but I aggregated 5035 entries.  One is backslash formatted (f.e. "C:\Windows\System32\") for Windows native operations and the other is forward slash formatted (f.e. "C:/Windows/System32/") for the more common attack techniques (such as URL-based); if the duplication isn't desired, the latter forward slash wordlist would probably provide more universal usage.  Capitalization is fairly normalized to default Windows and software folder/file structures.

[Windows-Files-Backslash.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25781805/Windows-Files-Backslash.txt)
[Windows-Files-Forward-Slash.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25781851/Windows-Files-Forward-Slash.txt)

**Additional context**:
I don't claim to have originated the content.  The entries were predominantly sourced from the soffensive list, with the gracefulsecurity and sirensecurity appended before dedup'ing.

https://raw.githubusercontent.com/soffensive/windowsblindread/refs/heads/master/windows-files.txt
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/danielmiessler/SecLists/refs/heads/master/Fuzzing/LFI/LFI-gracefulsecurity-windows.txt
https://sirensecurity.io/blog/file-inclusion-reference/

**Next steps**:
Not well versed in GIT operations, so I opted for creating this Issue as the FR, instead of attempting and mangling a pull request.  Thank you in advanced for the consideration to incorporating these.

- [ ] I intend to open a pull request later

title: Add auto-updater for API fuzzing wordlists
author: ItsIgnacioPortal
body:
**Describe the feature request**:
The README at `Discovery\Web-Content\api\README.md` states that the `api-endpoints-res.txt` wordlist is composed of several other wordlists. This should be auto-updating, like we have done with other wordlists through a github actions pipeline.

title: Language-specific web content-discovery wordlists
author: ItsIgnacioPortal
body:
**Describe the feature request**:
> Even though English is the most used language, some targets still use their own language in their application; for this reason, it's necessary to use wordlists adapted for this context.

The prospect of language-specific wordlists for things other than passwords seems interesting. But this might be difficult to develop without assistance from people that speak each language.

At least for web content-discovery, for every single line we should consider whether it comes from default filenames generated by software, or human-picked filenames. To generate each "final" language-specific wordlist we would combine the wordlist of default filenames with a wordlist of language-specific human-picked filenames, like we have done with the automatically-updating Discovery/Web-Content/combined_directories.txt wordlist.

We should also consider issue https://github.com/danielmiessler/SecLists/issues/1264. Also related, issue #1210 

**Next steps**:
- [X] I intend to open a pull request later

title: [Bug report]: Corrupted ZIP file in release/download
author: realpug
body:
**Describe the bug**:

When using the command from the installations Description:

```
$ wget -c https://github.com/danielmiessler/SecLists/archive/master.zip -O SecList.zip && unzip SecList.zip && rm -f SecList.zip
```

we encounter the following issue:

```
$ unzip SecList
Archive:  SecList.zip
  End-of-central-directory signature not found.  Either this file is not
  a zipfile, or it constitutes one disk of a multi-part archive.  In the
  latter case the central directory and zipfile comment will be found on
  the last disk(s) of this archive.
unzip:  cannot find zipfile directory in one of SecList or
        SecList.zip, and cannot find SecList.ZIP, period.
```

it can be fixed with this workaround:

```
$ zip -FF SecList.zip --out repaired.zip
```

further we are now able to unzip it:

```
$ unzip repaired.zip 
<snip>...
```

title: [Feature request]: Locale and language ISO codes
author: ghost
body:
**Describe the feature request**:
A list of the possible ISO 3166 and ISO 639 locale and language codes.

**Additional context**:
These are quite simple lists compiled from [Simple Localize](https://simplelocalize.io/data/), but can be quite useful (e.g. when trying to brute-force services that perform geo-location).

[languages-codes.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/23780683/languages-codes.txt)
[locale-codes.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/23780684/locale-codes.txt)

**Next steps**:
Since this is such a simple addition, I think that there is no need to open a pull request just for it.

title: [Bug report]: Potential dangerous line in file `wso2-enterprise-integrator.txt`
author: Maltemo
body:
I discovered while using the wordlist `wso2-enterprise-integrator.txt` that one of its entry tries to exploit what seems to be a SSRF to a (external or local ?) server :
```
carbon/wsdl2code/index.jsp?generateClient=http://320932f0d81e:8280/services/wso2carbon-sts?wsdl&toppage=false&endpoints=wso2carbon-stsHttpsSoap11Endpoint,wso2carbon-stsHttpsSoap12Endpoint
```
[At line 68 of file `/Discovery/Web-Content/wso2-enterprise-integrator.txt`](https://github.com/danielmiessler/SecLists/blob/1736552927665e31de3be080c3064490c72ad836/Discovery/Web-Content/wso2-enterprise-integrator.txt#L68)

I tried to decode the IP encoded in Hex and got two different results depending of which converter I used and the size of the hexadecimal value didn’t seem to be standard.

In my opinion, if this value decodes to localhost or 0.0.0.0, this entry isn’t a problem. But if it decodes to an external IP, this could be dangerous for everybody using this wordlist and having a vulnerable WSO2 instance, pinging back to this potential external IP.

@righettod you added `wso2-enterprise-integrator.txt` file with this entry, maybe you could help me to understand if I misunderstood this line ?

title: Add Kubernetes wordlists
author: ItsIgnacioPortal

Add the wordlist `important_endpoints.txt` from https://github.com/manojdeshmukh45/kubernetes_wordlist into `Discovery\Web-Content\quickhits.txt`. (Related to #1264)

**Additional context**:
Related to #1260

title: Modularize and change format of quickhits.txt
author: ItsIgnacioPortal
body:
**Describe the feature request**:
Similar to how `Discovery/Web-Content/combined_words.txt` and `Discovery/Web-Content/combined_directories` are composed of several other wordlists, it would be beneficial to modularize the quickhits.txt wordlist so it can contain automatically-updating components.

Using a different wordlist format should also be considered so that when the wordlist is used, it's easier to trace back which web service the matched URL path corresponds to.

**Next steps**:
Proposals for what that new format should look like are welcome.
